g.data-narration: Welcome! In this lecture, we'll learn how to implement a Queue using Arrays, a fundamental data structure.
A queue is a FIFO structure - First In, First Out. Think of a line at a ticket counter.
g.text: FIFO: First element inserted is first element removed

g.data-narration: We use a fixed-size array to store queue elements. Two pointers track the front and rear of the queue.
Initially, both front and rear are set to -1, indicating an empty queue with no elements.
When adding the first element, we set front to 0 and rear to 0, then insert at queue[rear].
Adding more elements increments rear and stores values. Both front and rear advance together.
Dequeue removes from the front. We retrieve queue[front], then increment front to mark removal.
Overflow occurs when rear reaches MAX-1 (4 in our 5-element array), indicating the queue is full.
Underflow occurs when trying to dequeue from an empty queue - when front > rear.
This is the key problem! After dequeuing, front advances but space at the beginning cannot be reused.
Circular Queue solves this by wrapping rear back to the beginning when it reaches the end, reusing space!
Let's summarize what we learned about implementing queues using arrays and the importance of circular queues.
